revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.
revision.text: #redirect[[en:September (旧暦)]] <noinclude>[[Category:New automatic wikipedia redirects]]</noinclude><includeonly>[[Category:Erroneous transclusions of September (旧暦)]]</includeonly>

'''旧暦9月'''は、[[旧暦]]（[[太陰太陽暦]]）の年初から9番目の[[月 (暦)|月]]である。
[[天保暦]]よりも前の定義では、[[霜降]]を含む月を9月とする。[[新暦]]では[[9月]]下旬から[[11月]]上旬ごろに当たる。
9月の別名は'''長月'''（'''ながづき'''）である。名前の由来は[[9月]]を参照のこと。

revision.text:
'''旧暦9月'''（きゅうれきくがつ）は、[[旧暦]]（[[太陰太陽暦]]）の年初から9番目の[[月 (暦)|月]]である。
[[天保暦]]よりも前の定義では、[[霜降]]を含む月を9月とする。[[新暦]]では[[9月]]下旬から[[11月]]上旬ごろに当たる。
9月の別名は'''長月'''（'''ながづき'''）である。名前の由来は[[9月]]を参照のこと。
東洋の太陰太陽暦では月の日数である[[大小]]（大月30日、小月29日）が年により異なるため、9月29日までで9月30日は存在しない年もある。

revision.text:
{{Lunar September}} '''Lunar September'''（きゅうれきくがつ）は、[[旧暦]]（[[太陰太陽暦]]）の年初から9番目の[[月 (暦)|月]]である。
[[天保暦]]よりも前の定義では、[[霜降]]を含む月を9月とする。[[新暦]]では[[9月]]下旬から[[11月]]上旬ごろに当たる。
9月の別名は'''長月'''（ながづき）である。名前の由来は[[9月]]を参照のこと。異名は「きしゅう（季秋）」。
東洋の太陰太陽暦では月の日数である[[大小]]（大月30日、小月29日）が年により異なるため、9月29日までで9月30日は存在しない年もある。

{{Lunar September}} '''Lunar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月}} of the year in the {{ill|Old Lunar Calendar|ja|旧暦}} ({{ill|Lunisolar Calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Frost Descent|ja|霜降}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|ja|9月}} to early {{ill|November|ja|11月}}.
Another name for September is {{reading|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき}}. The origin of the name is explained in {{ill|September|ja|9月}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.

In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.

{{Lunar September}} '''Lunar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月}} of the year in the {{ill|Old Lunar Calendar|ja|旧暦}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Frost Descent|ja|霜降}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|ja|9月}} to early {{ill|November|ja|11月}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき}}. The origin of the name is explained in {{ill|September|ja|9月}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.

Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Frost Descent|ja|霜降}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|ja|9月}} to early {{ill|November|ja|11月}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき}}. The origin of the name is explained in {{ill|September|ja|9月}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.

Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき}}. The origin of the name is explained in {{ill|September|ja|9月}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
== Nagatsuki Dates == {| class="wikitable" |+ Nagatsuki Dates ! Gregorian Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! {{Lunar September}} '''Lunar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Frost Descent|ja|霜降}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|ja|9月}} to early {{ill|November|ja|11月}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき}}. The origin of the name is explained in {{ill|September|ja|9月}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
== Nagatsuki Dates == {| class="wikitable" |+ Nagatsuki Dates ! Gregorian Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! Number of Days |- | 2014 || {{ill|September 24|ja|9月24日}} || {{ill|October 23|ja|10月23日}} || 30 days |- | 2014 ([[Leap month|leap]]) || {{ill|October 24|ja|10月24日}} || {{ill|November 21|ja|11月21日}} || 29 days |- | 2015 || {{ill|October 13|ja|10月13日}} || {{ill|November 11|ja|11月11日}} || 30 days |- | 2016 || {{ill|October 1|ja|10月1日}} || {{ill|October 30|ja|10月30日}} || 30 days |- | 2017 || {{ill|October 20|ja|10月20日}} || {{ill|November 17|ja|11月17日}} || 29 days |- | 2018 || {{ill|October 9|ja|10月9日}} || {{ill|November 7|ja|11月7日}} || 30 days |- | 2019 || {{ill|September 29|ja|9月29日}} || {{ill|October 27|ja|10月27日}} || 29 days |- | 2020 || {{ill|October 17|ja|10月17日}} || {{ill|November 14|ja|11月14日}} || 29 days |- | 2021 || {{ill|October 6|ja|10月6日}} || {{ill|November 4|ja|11月4日}} || 30 days |- | 2022 || {{ill|September 26|ja|9月26日}} || {{ill|October 24|ja|10月24日}} || 29 days |- | 2023 || {{ill|October 15|ja|10月15日}} || {{ill|November 12|ja|11月12日}} || 29 days |- | 2024 || {{ill|October 3|ja|10月3日}} || {{ill|October 31|ja|10月31日}} || 29 days |- | 2025 || {{ill|October 21|ja|10月21日}} || {{ill|November 19|ja|11月19日}} || 30 days |}

In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.

revision.text:
{{Lunisolar September}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月|12=draft}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=draft}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦|12=draft}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦|12=draft}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Frost Descent|ja|霜降|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|12=draft}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|ja|9月|12=draft}} to early {{ill|November|ja|11月|12=draft}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき}}. The origin of the name is explained in {{ill|September|ja|9月|12=draft}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=draft}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.

revision.text:
{{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦|1=Lunisolar calendar|2=ja|3=太陰太陽暦|comment=note that this is not the Japanese lunisolar calendar it is any lunisolar calendar|4=ru|5=Лунно-солнечный календарь|6=de|7=Lunisolarkalender|8=es|9=Calendario lunisolar|10=fr|11=Calendrier luni-solaire|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Frost Descent|ja|霜降|1=Shuangjiang (solar term)|2=ja|3=霜降|lt=Shuangjiang|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}} to early {{ill|November|ja|11月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Hokkaido Ainu language|ja|北海道アイヌ語}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|ja|アイヌ語}}: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before {{ill|July|ja|7月}} was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by {{ill|Gaius Julius Caesar|ja|ガイウス・ユリウス・カエサル}}, it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度}} and {{ill|fiscal year|ja|会計年度}} mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the {{ill|United States|ja|アメリカ合衆国}}, {{ill|Canada|ja|カナダ}}, {{ill|Europe|ja|ヨーロッパ}}, and the {{ill|People's Republic of China|ja|中華人民共和国}}).
September begins on the same {{ill|day of the week|ja|曜日}} as {{ill|December|ja|12月}} of that year.
== Nagatsuki Dates == {| class="wikitable" |+ Nagatsuki Dates ! Gregorian Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! revision.text:
{{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦|1=Lunisolar calendar|2=ja|3=太陰太陽暦|comment=note that this is not the Japanese lunisolar calendar it is any lunisolar calendar|4=ru|5=Лунно-солнечный календарь|6=de|7=Lunisolarkalender|8=es|9=Calendario lunisolar|10=fr|11=Calendrier luni-solaire|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Frost Descent|ja|霜降|1=Shuangjiang (solar term)|2=ja|3=霜降|lt=Shuangjiang|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}} to early {{ill|November|ja|11月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Hokkaido Ainu language|ja|北海道アイヌ語}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|ja|アイヌ語}}: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before {{ill|July|ja|7月}} was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by {{ill|Gaius Julius Caesar|ja|ガイウス・ユリウス・カエサル}}, it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度}} and {{ill|fiscal year|ja|会計年度}} mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the {{ill|United States|ja|アメリカ合衆国}}, {{ill|Canada|ja|カナダ}}, {{ill|Europe|ja|ヨーロッパ}}, and the {{ill|People's Republic of China|ja|中華人民共和国}}).
September begins on the same {{ill|day of the week|ja|曜日|1=Day of the week|2=ja|3=曜日|qq=}} as {{ill|December|ja|12月}} of that year.

{{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Month (Calendar)|qq=}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦|1=Lunisolar calendar|2=ja|3=太陰太陽暦|comment=note that this is not the Japanese lunisolar calendar it is any lunisolar calendar|4=ru|5=Лунно-солнечный календарь|6=de|7=Lunisolarkalender|8=es|9=Calendario lunisolar|10=fr|11=Calendrier luni-solaire|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar calendar|qq=}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō Calendar|qq=}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Frost Descent|ja|霜降|1=Shuangjiang (solar term)|2=ja|3=霜降|lt=Shuangjiang|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Frost Descent|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuangjiang (solar term)|qq=}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September|qq=}} to early {{ill|November|ja|11月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November|qq=}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}}, the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Hokkaido Ainu language|ja|北海道アイヌ語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido Ainu language|qq=}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|ja|アイヌ語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu language|qq=}}: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before {{ill|July|ja|7月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July|qq=}} was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by {{ill|Gaius Julius Caesar|ja|ガイウス・ユリウス・カエサル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaius Julius Caesar|qq=}}, it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}}, the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|qq=}} and {{ill|fiscal year|ja|会計年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fiscal year|qq=}} mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the {{ill|United States|ja|アメリカ合衆国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/United States|qq=}}, {{ill|Canada|ja|カナダ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Canada|qq=}}, {{ill|Europe|ja|ヨーロッパ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Europe|qq=}}, and the {{ill|People's Republic of China|ja|中華人民共和国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/People's Republic of China|qq=}}).
September begins on the same {{ill|day of the week|ja|曜日|1=Day of the week|2=ja|3=曜日|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/day of the week|13=User:Immanuelle/Day of the week|qq=}} as {{ill|December|ja|12月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} of that year.

revision.text:
{{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Month (Calendar)|qq=}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦|1=Lunisolar calendar|2=ja|3=太陰太陽暦|comment=note that this is not the Japanese lunisolar calendar it is any lunisolar calendar|4=ru|5=Лунно-солнечный календарь|6=de|7=Lunisolarkalender|8=es|9=Calendario lunisolar|10=fr|11=Calendrier luni-solaire|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunisolar calendar|qq=}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō Calendar|qq=}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Frost Descent|ja|霜降|1=Shuangjiang (solar term)|2=ja|3=霜降|lt=Shuangjiang|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Frost Descent|13=User:Immanuelle/Shuangjiang (solar term)|qq=}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September|qq=}} to early {{ill|November|ja|11月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November|qq=}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}}, the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Hokkaido Ainu language|ja|北海道アイヌ語|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language|2=ja|3=アイヌ語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido Ainu language|qq=}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|ja|アイヌ語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu language|qq=}}: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before {{ill|July|ja|7月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July|qq=}} was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by {{ill|Gaius Julius Caesar|ja|ガイウス・ユリウス・カエサル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaius Julius Caesar|qq=}}, it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}}, the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|qq=}} and {{ill|fiscal year|ja|会計年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fiscal year|qq=}} mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the {{ill|United States|ja|アメリカ合衆国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/United States|qq=}}, {{ill|Canada|ja|カナダ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Canada|qq=}}, {{ill|Europe|ja|ヨーロッパ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Europe|qq=}}, and the {{ill|People's Republic of China|ja|中華人民共和国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/People's Republic of China|qq=}}).
September begins on the same {{ill|day of the week|ja|曜日|1=Day of the week|2=ja|3=曜日|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/day of the week|13=User:Immanuelle/Day of the week|qq=}} as {{ill|December|ja|12月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December|qq=}} of that year.

revision.text:
{{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Hokkaido Ainu language|ja|北海道アイヌ語|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language|2=ja|3=アイヌ語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido Ainu language|qq=}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|qq=}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same {{ill|day of the week|ja|曜日|1=Day of the week|2=ja|3=曜日|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/day of the week|13=User:Immanuelle/Day of the week|qq=}} as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

{{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Hokkaido Ainu language|ja|北海道アイヌ語|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language|2=ja|3=アイヌ語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido Ainu language|qq=}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|qq=}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same {{ill|day of the week|ja|曜日|1=Day of the week|2=ja|3=曜日|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/day of the week|13=User:Immanuelle/Day of the week|qq=}} as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Hokkaido Ainu language|ja|北海道アイヌ語|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language|2=ja|3=アイヌ語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido Ainu language|qq=}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|qq=}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same {{ill|day of the week|ja|曜日|1=Day of the week|2=ja|3=曜日|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/day of the week|13=User:Immanuelle/Day of the week|qq=}} as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

{{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.

The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Hokkaido Ainu language|ja|北海道アイヌ語|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language|2=ja|3=アイヌ語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido Ainu language|qq=}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.

Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>

September begins on the same {{ill|day of the week|ja|曜日|1=Day of the week|2=ja|3=曜日|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/day of the week|13=User:Immanuelle/Day of the week|qq=}} as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

{{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q840528]] Wikidata name: 9th month [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].

Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh_min_nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh_yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q840528]] Wikidata name: 9th month [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk hak]] [[gan:九月]] [[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍tja]] [[wuu:农历九月]] [[de:September (Lunisolar)]]
[[ja:9月 (旧暦)]]
[[ko:음력 9월]] [[th:เดือน 9]] [[vi:Tháng chín]] [[za:Ndwengiau]] [[zh:农历九月]] [[ar:September (Lunisolar)]] <references group="lower-alpha"/> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]] [[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]] [[za:Ndwengouj]] [[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]] [[zh-yue:九月]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]] [[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]] [[za:Ndwengouj]] [[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]] [[zh-yue:九月]] {{wikidata link|Q840528}} [[Category:Pages linked to by wikidata]] [[Category:Non matching pagename on wikidata]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q840528]] Wikidata name: 9th month [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q840528]] Wikidata name: 9th month [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=draft|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.

The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).

Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.
