revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q840528]] Wikidata name: 9th month [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.
== Nagatsuki Dates == {| class="wikitable" |+ Nagatsuki Dates ! Gregorian Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! Number of Days |- | 2014 || [[:en:September 24|September 24]] || [[:en:October 23|October 23]] || 30 days |- | 2014 ([[:en:Leap month|leap]]) || [[:en:October 24|October 24]] || [[:en:November 21|November 21]] || 29 days |- | 2015 || [[:en:October 13|October 13]] || [[:en:November 11|November 11]] || 30 days |- | 2016 || [[:en:October 1|October 1]] || [[:en:October 30|October 30]] || 30 days |- | 2017 || [[:en:October 20|October 20]] || [[:en:November 17|November 17]] || 29 days |- | 2018 || [[:en:October 9|October 9]] || [[:en:November 7|November 7]] || 30 days |- | 2019 || [[:en:September 29|September 29]] || [[:en:October 27|October 27]] || 29 days |- | 2020 || [[:en:October 17|October 17]] || [[:en:November 14|November 14]] || 29 days |- | 2021 || [[:en:October 6|October 6]] || [[:en:November 4|November 4]] || 30 days |- | 2022 || [[:en:September 26|September 26]] || [[:en:October 24|October 24]] || 29 days |- | 2023 || [[:en:October 15|October 15]] || [[:en:November 12|November 12]] || 29 days |- | 2024 || [[:en:October 3|October 3]] || [[:en:October 31|October 31]] || 29 days |- | 2025 || [[:en:October 21|October 21]] || [[:en:November 19|November 19]] || 30 days |}

In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.

[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk hak]] [[gan:九月]] [[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍tja]] [[wuu:农历九月]] [[de:September (Lunisolar)]]
[[ja:9月 (旧暦)]]
[[ko:음력 9월]] [[th:เดือน 9]] [[vi:Tháng chín]] [[za:Ndwengiau]] [[zh:农历九月]] [[ar:September (Lunisolar)]] <references group="lower-alpha"/> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]] [[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]] [[za:Ndwengouj]] [[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]] [[zh-yue:九月]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]] [[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]] [[za:Ndwengouj]] [[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]] [[zh-yue:九月]] {{wikidata link|Q840528}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q840528]] Wikidata name: 9th month [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q840528]] Wikidata name: 9th month [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).

In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].

In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.

The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh_min_nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh_yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q840528]] Wikidata name: 9th month [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.

Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>

September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh_min_nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh_yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh_min_nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh_yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q840528]] Wikidata name: 9th month [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].

In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.

In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh_min_nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh_yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh_min_nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh_yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q840528]] Wikidata name: 9th month [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q840528]] Wikidata name: 9th month [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions preceding the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], September is defined as the month that includes [[:en:Shuangjiang (solar term)|Shuangjiang]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:September|September]] to early [[:en:November|November]].
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before [[:en:July|July]] was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by [[:en:Julius Caesar|Gaius Julius Caesar]], it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and [[:en:Fiscal year|fiscal year]] mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the [[:en:United States|United States]], [[:en:Canada|Canada]], [[:en:Europe|Europe]], and the [[:en:China|People's Republic of China]]).
September begins on the same [[:en:Day of the week|day of the week]] as [[:en:December|December]] of that year.

Number of Days |- | 2014 || [[:en:September 24|September 24]] || [[:en:October 23|October 23]] || 30 days |- | 2014 ([[:en:Leap month|leap]]) || [[:en:October 24|October 24]] || [[:en:November 21|November 21]] || 29 days |- | 2015 || [[:en:October 13|October 13]] || [[:en:November 11|November 11]] || 30 days |- | 2016 || [[:en:October 1|October 1]] || [[:en:October 30|October 30]] || 30 days |- | 2017 || [[:en:October 20|October 20]] || [[:en:November 17|November 17]] || 29 days |- | 2018 || [[:en:October 9|October 9]] || [[:en:November 7|November 7]] || 30 days |- | 2019 || [[:en:September 29|September 29]] || [[:en:October 27|October 27]] || 29 days |- | 2020 || [[:en:October 17|October 17]] || [[:en:November 14|November 14]] || 29 days |- | 2021 || [[:en:October 6|October 6]] || [[:en:November 4|November 4]] || 30 days |- | 2022 || [[:en:September 26|September 26]] || [[:en:October 24|October 24]] || 29 days |- | 2023 || [[:en:October 15|October 15]] || [[:en:November 12|November 12]] || 29 days |- | 2024 || [[:en:October 3|October 3]] || [[:en:October 31|October 31]] || 29 days |- | 2025 || [[:en:October 21|October 21]] || [[:en:November 19|November 19]] || 30 days |}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q840528]] Wikidata name: 9th month [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Shuangjiang (solar term)|en|Shuangjiang (solar term)|WD=Q1147794|lt=Shuangjiang}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|en|September|WD=Q123|lt=September}} to early {{ill|November|en|November|WD=Q125|lt=November}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July}} was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by {{ill|Julius Caesar|en|Julius Caesar|WD=Q1048|lt=Gaius Julius Caesar}}, it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and {{ill|Fiscal year|en|Fiscal year|WD=Q191891|lt=fiscal year}} mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the {{ill|United States|en|United States|WD=Q30|lt=United States}}, {{ill|Canada|en|Canada|WD=Q16|lt=Canada}}, {{ill|Europe|en|Europe|WD=Q46|lt=Europe}}, and the {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=People's Republic of China}}).
September begins on the same {{ill|Day of the week|en|Day of the week|WD=Q41825|lt=day of the week}} as {{ill|December|en|December|WD=Q126|lt=December}} of that year.

In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|en|September|WD=Q123|lt=September}} to early {{ill|November|en|November|WD=Q125|lt=November}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July}} was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by {{ill|Julius Caesar|en|Julius Caesar|WD=Q1048|lt=Gaius Julius Caesar}}, it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and {{ill|Fiscal year|en|Fiscal year|WD=Q191891|lt=fiscal year}} mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the {{ill|United States|en|United States|WD=Q30|lt=United States}}, {{ill|Canada|en|Canada|WD=Q16|lt=Canada}}, {{ill|Europe|en|Europe|WD=Q46|lt=Europe}}, and the {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=People's Republic of China}}).
September begins on the same {{ill|Day of the week|en|Day of the week|WD=Q41825|lt=day of the week}} as {{ill|December|en|December|WD=Q126|lt=December}} of that year.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q840528]] Wikidata name: 9th month [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Shuangjiang (solar term)|en|Shuangjiang (solar term)|WD=Q1147794|lt=Shuangjiang}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|en|September|WD=Q123|lt=September}} to early {{ill|November|en|November|WD=Q125|lt=November}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:9月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:43, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July}} was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by {{ill|Julius Caesar|en|Julius Caesar|WD=Q1048|lt=Gaius Julius Caesar}}, it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and {{ill|Fiscal year|en|Fiscal year|WD=Q191891|lt=fiscal year}} mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the {{ill|United States|en|United States|WD=Q30|lt=United States}}, {{ill|Canada|en|Canada|WD=Q16|lt=Canada}}, {{ill|Europe|en|Europe|WD=Q46|lt=Europe}}, and the {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=People's Republic of China}}).
September begins on the same {{ill|Day of the week|en|Day of the week|WD=Q41825|lt=day of the week}} as {{ill|December|en|December|WD=Q126|lt=December}} of that year.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]]
{{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Shuangjiang (solar term)|en|Shuangjiang (solar term)|WD=Q1147794|lt=Shuangjiang}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|en|September|WD=Q123|lt=September}} to early {{ill|November|en|November|WD=Q125|lt=November}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July}} was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by {{ill|Julius Caesar|en|Julius Caesar|WD=Q1048|lt=Gaius Julius Caesar}}, it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and {{ill|Fiscal year|en|Fiscal year|WD=Q191891|lt=fiscal year}} mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the {{ill|United States|en|United States|WD=Q30|lt=United States}}, {{ill|Canada|en|Canada|WD=Q16|lt=Canada}}, {{ill|Europe|en|Europe|WD=Q46|lt=Europe}}, and the {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=People's Republic of China}}).
September begins on the same {{ill|Day of the week|en|Day of the week|WD=Q41825|lt=day of the week}} as {{ill|December|en|December|WD=Q126|lt=December}} of that year.

{{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Shuangjiang (solar term)|en|Shuangjiang (solar term)|WD=Q1147794|lt=Shuangjiang}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|en|September|WD=Q123|lt=September}} to early {{ill|November|en|November|WD=Q125|lt=November}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July}} was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by {{ill|Julius Caesar|en|Julius Caesar|WD=Q1048|lt=Gaius Julius Caesar}}, it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and {{ill|Fiscal year|en|Fiscal year|WD=Q191891|lt=fiscal year}} mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the {{ill|United States|en|United States|WD=Q30|lt=United States}}, {{ill|Canada|en|Canada|WD=Q16|lt=Canada}}, {{ill|Europe|en|Europe|WD=Q46|lt=Europe}}, and the {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=People's Republic of China}}).
September begins on the same {{ill|Day of the week|en|Day of the week|WD=Q41825|lt=day of the week}} as {{ill|December|en|December|WD=Q126|lt=December}} of that year.

Number of Days |- | 2014 || {{ill|September 24|en|September 24|WD=Q2890|lt=September 24}} || {{ill|October 23|en|October 23|WD=Q2958|lt=October 23}} || 30 days |- | 2014 ([[:en:Leap month|leap]]) || {{ill|October 24|en|October 24|WD=Q2955|lt=October 24}} || {{ill|November 21|en|November 21|WD=Q3023|lt=November 21}} || 29 days |- | 2015 || {{ill|October 13|en|October 13|WD=Q2925|lt=October 13}} || {{ill|November 11|en|November 11|WD=Q2987|lt=November 11}} || 30 days |- | 2016 || {{ill|October 1|en|October 1|WD=Q2913|lt=October 1}} || {{ill|October 30|en|October 30|WD=Q2950|lt=October 30}} || 30 days |- | 2017 || {{ill|October 20|en|October 20|WD=Q2959|lt=October 20}} || {{ill|November 17|en|November 17|WD=Q3028|lt=November 17}} || 29 days |- | 2018 || {{ill|October 9|en|October 9|WD=Q2923|lt=October 9}} || {{ill|November 7|en|November 7|WD=Q2989|lt=November 7}} || 30 days |- | 2019 || {{ill|September 29|en|September 29|WD=Q2882|lt=September 29}} || {{ill|October 27|en|October 27|WD=Q2956|lt=October 27}} || 29 days |- | 2020 || {{ill|October 17|en|October 17|WD=Q2962|lt=October 17}} || {{ill|November 14|en|November 14|WD=Q2983|lt=November 14}} || 29 days |- | 2021 || {{ill|October 6|en|October 6|WD=Q2928|lt=October 6}} || {{ill|November 4|en|November 4|WD=Q2994|lt=November 4}} || 30 days |- | 2022 || {{ill|September 26|en|September 26|WD=Q2885|lt=September 26}} || {{ill|October 24|en|October 24|WD=Q2955|lt=October 24}} || 29 days |- | 2023 || {{ill|October 15|en|October 15|WD=Q2919|lt=October 15}} || {{ill|November 12|en|November 12|WD=Q2986|lt=November 12}} || 29 days |- | 2024 || {{ill|October 3|en|October 3|WD=Q2931|lt=October 3}} || {{ill|October 31|en|October 31|WD=Q2949|lt=October 31}} || 29 days |- | 2025 || {{ill|October 21|en|October 21|WD=Q2960|lt=October 21}} || {{ill|November 19|en|November 19|WD=Q3026|lt=November 19}} || 30 days |}

In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July|1=July}} was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by {{ill|Julius Caesar|en|Julius Caesar|WD=Q1048|lt=Gaius Julius Caesar|1=Julius Caesar}}, it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and {{ill|Fiscal year|en|Fiscal year|WD=Q191891|lt=fiscal year|1=Fiscal year}} mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the {{ill|United States|en|United States|WD=Q30|lt=United States|1=United States}}, {{ill|Canada|en|Canada|WD=Q16|lt=Canada|1=Canada}}, {{ill|Europe|en|Europe|WD=Q46|lt=Europe|1=Europe}}, and the {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=People's Republic of China|1=China}}).
September begins on the same {{ill|Day of the week|en|Day of the week|WD=Q41825|lt=day of the week|1=Day of the week}} as {{ill|December|en|December|WD=Q126|lt=December|1=December}} of that year.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 9 nguŏk]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 9-ngie̍t]][[ja:9月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 9월]][[th:เดือน 9]][[za:Ndwengouj]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 9 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:九月]][[zh:农历九月]]
{{Lunisolar September|qq=}} '''Lunisolar September''' (旧暦九月) is the ninth {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions preceding the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}}, September is defined as the month that includes {{ill|Shuangjiang (solar term)|en|Shuangjiang (solar term)|WD=Q1147794|lt=Shuangjiang|1=Shuangjiang (solar term)}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|September|en|September|WD=Q123|lt=September|1=September}} to early {{ill|November|en|November|WD=Q125|lt=November|1=November}}.
Another name for September is {{nihongo|'''Nagatsuki'''|ながづき|qq=}}. The origin of the name is explained in [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]{{efn|September|ja|9月|qq=}}. An alternative name is “Kishū (季秋)”.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, because the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}} (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days), varies by year, there are years when September ends on the 29th and no September 30th exists.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the {{ill|September (Lunisolar)|ja|9月 (旧暦)|lt=Lunisolar September|lt_ja=旧暦9月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September (Lunisolar)|qq=}} is called '''Nagatsuki''' (ながつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for September of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. The most widely accepted theory holds that it is an abbreviation of “Yonanagatsuki” (夜長月; よながつき), “long-night month.”<ref name="kotobank">{{Cite web |title=長月(ナガツキ)とは？ 意味や使い方 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%95%B7%E6%9C%88-587894 |website=コトバンク |access-date=2024-12-01 |language=ja |first=デジタル大辞泉,精選版 |last=日本国語大辞典,日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ),とっさの日本語便利帳}}</ref> Other theories propose that it derives from variations of “Inaagaritsuki” (稲熟月; いなあがりつき) or “Inakaritsuki” (稲刈月; いなかりつき). Another alternate name is “Nezametsuki” (寝覚月; ねざめつき).
In the Asahikawa dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}, September is called “Yas chup” (ヤㇱチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: yas cup), meaning “month of drift-net fishing”<ref>[https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 《ことばからみたアイヌ文化と自然２》アイヌ文化における時間使用 高橋 靖以（北海道大学アイヌ・先住民研究センター博士研究員＝特別寄稿） 月刊シロㇿ]</ref>.
In English, the month name {{lang|en|September}} is identical in Latin, derived from the word {{lang|la|septem}} meaning “seven.” The mismatch arose because in 153 BCE the first month of the year was changed from March to January, yet the names were not updated—the popular notion that the shift occurred due to naming July and August after emperors is a myth. Before {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July|1=July}} was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by {{ill|Julius Caesar|en|Julius Caesar|WD=Q1048|lt=Gaius Julius Caesar|1=Julius Caesar}}, it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and {{ill|Fiscal year|en|Fiscal year|WD=Q191891|lt=fiscal year|1=Fiscal year}} mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the {{ill|United States|en|United States|WD=Q30|lt=United States|1=United States}}, {{ill|Canada|en|Canada|WD=Q16|lt=Canada|1=Canada}}, {{ill|Europe|en|Europe|WD=Q46|lt=Europe|1=Europe}}, and the {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=People's Republic of China|1=China}}).
September begins on the same {{ill|Day of the week|en|Day of the week|WD=Q41825|lt=day of the week|1=Day of the week}} as {{ill|December|en|December|WD=Q126|lt=December|1=December}} of that year.

Before {{ill|July|en|July|WD=Q121|lt=July|1=July}} was renamed {{lang|la|Julius}} from {{lang|la|Quintilis}} by {{ill|Julius Caesar|en|Julius Caesar|WD=Q1048|lt=Gaius Julius Caesar|1=Julius Caesar}}, it was called {{lang|la|Quintilis}}, from {{lang|la|quintus}} meaning “fifth,” showing the offset predates those changes.<ref>同様に一般的な暦の10月,11月,12月はそれぞれローマ暦で8,9,10番目の月にあたり、ラテン語の「第8の」「第9の」「第10の」を意味する {{lang|la|"octavius", "nonus", "decimus"}} に由来する</ref>
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the {{ill|school year|ja|学校年度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/school year|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 学校|1=School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3914}} and {{ill|Fiscal year|en|Fiscal year|WD=Q191891|lt=fiscal year|1=Fiscal year}} mostly start in April, but globally many countries adopt September (such as the {{ill|United States|en|United States|WD=Q30|lt=United States|1=United States}}, {{ill|Canada|en|Canada|WD=Q16|lt=Canada|1=Canada}}, {{ill|Europe|en|Europe|WD=Q46|lt=Europe|1=Europe}}, and the {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=People's Republic of China|1=China}}).
September begins on the same {{ill|Day of the week|en|Day of the week|WD=Q41825|lt=day of the week|1=Day of the week}} as {{ill|December|en|December|WD=Q126|lt=December|1=December}} of that year.
